segs.utf8: Welcome to Real Easy English, the podcast where

we have real conversations in easy 
English to help you learn. I'm Beth.

And I'm Neil. If you want to 
read along with this podcast,

you can find a full transcript on our 
website – BBC Learning English dot com.
How are you today, Neil?

I'm very well, thank you, Beth.
How about you?
I'm very good.

So what topic are we talking about
in this episode?

OK, well, today's episode
is all about habits and routines.

Now we talked about our favourite foods
a few weeks ago.

But today we'll talk about our meal times
and routines for eating.
Mm, sounds good!

What do we mean by a routine, Beth?
Is it different to a habit?

Well, they are similar, but a habit
is something we do regularly.

So, for example, I eat an apple
and a banana most days.

— Ooh, very healthy.
— Yeah.
A routine, though, is slightly different.

It's usually more about the time that we
do something and the way we do something.
So, I have my breakfast at 6am every day.
OK.

You said you have your breakfast
at 6am every day.
What do you usually have to eat?
Well, usually I have something simple.

A bowl of cereal,
some toast with marmalade.
How about you, Beth?
I usually have porridge.

And what about lunch?
What time do you have lunch, usually?

Mm, I normally have lunch
at about one o'clock. What about you?

Yeah, the same. I don't like it at 12.
That's too early.

But by two, I'm getting a little bit
hungry and angry at the same time!
There's a word for that, Neil.

So if you're hungry and angry,
we can combine them and say 'hangry'.

Yeah. So it's important to have regular
meal times, so you don't get hangry.

That's true. Now you've just reminded me
of another word which is 'brunch'.

Now, that's a combination of the words
'breakfast' and 'lunch'.
Do you ever have brunch, Neil?
I love brunch, especially at weekends.

Yeah. When I was at university, I used to
have brunch pretty much every day.
So I would meet up with my friends

and we would go to a café
and we would have brunch.

Beth, you said that you used to
have brunch at university.
Used to — what's that mean?

Well, 'used to' is used to talk about
a habit in the past,

so something you did regularly
in the past and don't do now.

Now, another way of talking about
past habits is to use would.

So if you remember, I said I would message
my friends and we would have brunch.

So 'used to' and 'would' are both used
to talk about past habits.
Do you ever miss a meal?

No. I think when I was at school,
many years ago,

I used to miss breakfast,
because I wanted to stay in bed.
What about you?

I don't like to miss meals,
because, like I said, I get hangry.

But I guess I also used to miss breakfast
when I was a teenager

and I needed to sleep for a huge amount of
time every night and get up really late.

Yeah, I think it's quite normal
and quite interesting
how your habits change as you get older.

For example, missing meals
and that sort of thing.
Mm.

Right, let's recap the vocabulary
we learned during the conversation.

We learnt 'routine', 'the time and way
we usually do something'.

We heard 'used to' which we use
to talk about 'a habit in the past'

and 'would', which we can also use
to talk about past habits.
And my favourite 'brunch',

which is 'eating breakfast and lunch'
at the same time'.

We also heard a really new word
in English,

which is a combination of two words,
'hungry' and 'angry'.
It's 'hangry'.

Thanks for joining us on Real 
Easy English. Visit our website

for more activities and courses 
to help you with your English.

In the next episode we'll be 
talking more about our hobbies.
See you then! Bye.
Goodbye!
